sections.text: Welcome back to AP Computer Science A. Module Six — arrays. This is the moment your programs stop juggling a handful of variables and start handling collections — thirty grades, a hundred names, a thousand sensor readings. By the end of this lesson you'll declare arrays, index into them, and traverse them with confidence.

Imagine tracking thirty students' grades. Do you really make thirty separate variables — g one, g two, g three, all the way to g thirty? That's a mess. Java gives you one container that holds all thirty values in order. That container is an array. Same type, fixed size, one name.

Three things to cover. One — how to declare and initialize arrays, both with a size and with literal values. Two — how indexing works and how to ask an array for its length. Three — how to traverse with both a standard for loop and the for-each loop, plus the patterns that show up over and over.

Two ways to declare. First — int bracket bracket grades equals new int bracket thirty bracket semicolon. That gives you thirty slots, all initialized to zero. Second — int bracket bracket xs equals open brace five, ten, fifteen close brace semicolon. The literal form. Java counts the values and sizes the array for you.

The literal form works for any type. String array names, set to Anya, Bo, and Cami in braces. Double array rates, set to point zero five and point zero seven five. The brace form is great for small, known-up-front collections — test data, configuration values, anything you can type out by hand.

Arrays are zero-indexed. For an array of size n, valid indices run from zero to n minus one. With xs holding ten, twenty, thirty — xs bracket zero is ten, xs bracket two is thirty. Ask for xs bracket three and Java throws an ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException at runtime. Off-by-one is the bug you'll hunt the most this year.

Every array knows its own size. Write arr dot length — no parentheses. That trips students up because String dot length has parentheses. Array dot length is a field, not a method. For an array of size three, dot length returns three, and the last valid index is dot length minus one. Memorize that pattern.

The standard for loop. For, open paren, int i equals zero, semicolon, i less than arr dot length, semicolon, i plus plus, close paren. Then inside, print arr bracket i. That's the workhorse. You start at zero, you go while i is strictly less than length, and you can modify arr at index i if you need to.

Your turn. Write a loop that sums every element of int array grades, storing the total in a variable called sum. Hint — start sum at zero, then loop through and add each element. Pause now, write the four or five lines, press play when you're ready.

Time. First, int sum equals zero. Then a standard for loop. For int i equals zero, while i less than grades dot length, i plus plus. And inside the loop, sum plus equals grades bracket i. After the loop, sum holds the total. Initialize, loop, accumulate. That's the running-total pattern you'll use everywhere.

The for-each loop. For, open paren, int g colon grades, close paren. Then inside, sum plus equals g. Each pass through the loop, g is the next element of grades. It's cleaner — but read-only. You can't reassign g and have it change grades. Use the indexed for loop when you need to write. Use for-each when you're just reading.

Three patterns that show up on every CS A exam. Max — track the largest value as you walk through. Count — increment a counter every time you see a match. Find — linear search, return the index when you find your target. Same skeleton — initialize, loop, check, update. Memorize the shape.

The classic bug. Writing arr bracket arr dot length to get the last element. That's wrong — the index length itself is out of bounds. Valid indices stop at length minus one. The correct last element is arr bracket arr dot length minus one. ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException is the number one array error you'll see all year. Burn this in.

Recap. Declare with new int bracket size bracket, or with a brace literal. Arrays are zero-indexed. Dot length is a field — no parentheses. Use the indexed for loop when you need to write to the array, for-each when you're only reading. The last index is always length minus one. Assignment in your dashboard — three short programs.

Reminder — this video is the overview. To actually master arrays, here's the rest. Read the Array chapter on Runestone — interactive code blocks make this stick. Practice on Codingbat — work through Array-One and Array-Two, fifteen problems total. Submit the assignment in your dashboard. Book a check-in with your advisor if traversal feels fuzzy. See you in Module Seven — ArrayList.
